Town of Southeast · Putnam County · New York

Southeast Centre

A Neighborhood History — Sodom Road & Brewster Hill Road
"Early colonial settlement on the Philipse Patent. Center of business prior to the coming of the Harlem Railroad and construction of the reservoirs. Early home of the Howes Family, circus performers and impresarios."
— Town of Southeast Historic Sites Commission marker

The Reckoning

Sodom Dam Proceeding — Chapter 490, Laws of 1883 — Awards published July 11, 1890

On July 11, 1890, the Brewster Standard published the commissioners' awards for the Sodom Dam watershed proceeding in full. The proceeding had displaced families, demolished buildings, and absorbed farmland across the entire East Branch of the Croton River watershed. The paper called the list "details of widespread interest." Fifty-six individual claimants and one railroad are named. Total awards: $257,801.27 — plus $80,000 to the New York & New England Railroad, paid the prior year.

The gap between what people believed their land was worth and what the commissioners awarded runs through every entry. Water power — the mill dams and races and privileges that had driven the neighborhood's economy since the 1740s — was the most contested element throughout. "Water power has proved to be a very knotty question throughout the whole proceeding," the paper observed.

53
Named claimants
13
Southeast Centre connections

1,471
Acres absorbed
Claimant Award Acres taken Notes
Stephen C. Barnum SE Centre $61,179.00 250+ ac Three generations of buildings; mill with water power; 20+ structures. Claimed $182,000.
Lydia M. Yale SE Centre $17,077.50 108 ac Yale farm of 130 acres; all buildings taken; 22 acres left. Husband paid $13,000 in 1858.
Melissa Birch SE Centre $15,700.00 128 ac 140-acre Howes family farm; farmhouse left standing then taken later. Refused $25,000 the year before.
Warren S. Paddock $12,260.00 75 ac 75 acres of meadow along the Croton above Milltown bridge; farm worth $30,000–$40,000 cut off from water.
Hiram Paddock $9,199.00 58 ac 156-acre farm; NY&NE Railroad now cuts through it; 19 acres stranded on Joe's Hills.
James W. Boyce $9,620.00 7 ac Milltown mill; machinery, dam, 47-acre farm. City put buildings at $2,439; claimant said $24,000.
The Hoyt Claim $9,600.00 ~15 ac David Ketchum property, ~30 acres; Putnam County Savings Bank and Henry B. Smith as mortgagees.
W.G. + J.J. Haviland $9,000.00 42 ac Bog Brook valley; 130-acre farm; reservoir will come within 10 feet of the residence.
George Cole SE Centre $8,306.83 37 ac 44-acre farm at Southeast Centre; residence, barn, wagon shop, water power. "Driven out of his home and away from his business."
Benjamin D. Everett $8,012.00 16 ac 78-acre farm; Bog Brook; Mary A. Field has $6,000 interest in the property.
Isaac Armstrong SE Centre $6,378.23 <0.5 ac Entire property taken at Southeast Centre — grist mill, sash and blinds manufactory, ice house. Water power owned jointly with Violette Birch.
Daniel H. Sears $6,626.00 28 ac 85-acre farm; 3 parcels; water supply cut off.
Elijah W. + Elizabeth A. Budd $6,010.00 ~32 ac 445-acre farm; 3 parcels taken.
Elbert H. Kelley $6,338.00 71 ac 76-acre farm; Minerva Howes holds a life estate. Nearly the entire farm taken.
John Martin $6,000.00 ~16 ac Farm near Milltown; buildings stripped. Claimant asked $11,000; city said $6,472.
Mary O'Connor $5,850.00 ~16 ac Farm near Milltown; 16 acres and buildings taken.
John Connor $4,291.00 12 ac Residence and 12 acres near Milltown. Award described as "highly satisfactory to the claimant."
Theodore B. Foster $4,883.01 many bldgs Little Sodom; many buildings. Zaoni D. Storm holds $2,700 mortgage.
Violette Birch SE Centre $4,260.00 3 ac Parcel along the spillway below the dam; brick building; one-half of the Armstrong water power. Submitted without testimony.
Martha M. Crosby SE Centre $4,804.00 12 ac 80-acre farm; 12 acres and a barn taken. Jacob O. Howes's daughter; wife of co-executor Seth O. Crosby.
Eli C. Barnum $3,450.00 43 ac East side of river above Milltown bridge. Submitted without testimony.
Catharine M. Crane $3,644.20 24 ac 216-acre farm at DeForest Corners; land cut in two and deprived of water.
Sarah K. + Lyman Sherwood $3,400.00 34 ac 47-acre farm; Bog Brook eastern slope.
Sarah E. + Delia Sherwood $3,600.00 20 ac 94-acre farm; Bog Brook eastern slope.
George Foster $3,375.00 2 ac Entire property taken at Little Sodom — dwelling, grist mill, saw mill, barn. City said $2,018; claimant said $12,000.
Benjamin F. Foster $3,800.00 18 ac 60-acre farm; Bog Brook eastern slope.
Sarah E. Paddock SE Centre $2,426.00 19 ac Wife of Warren S. Paddock; 23-acre parcel at the dam site; 19 acres taken.
O.J. + J.W. Hadden $2,250.00 16 ac 24.5-acre farm; city put it at $1,560; claimant asked $6,500.
Jonathan Shoor $2,300.00 8 ac 74-acre farm deprived of all springs and a brook by taking 8 acres.
Augustus Duane $1,900.00 15 ac 687-acre farm; 15 acres taken. City said $760; claimant said $11,000.
Herman C. Barnum $1,400.00 1 ac Residence and one acre; award paid to son Edward C. Barnum.
Elizabeth Dayton $1,500.00 — Dwelling.
Delsey Alford $766.00 — Dwelling.
W.F. Fowler + Alonzo Brush $1,396.50 10 ac 10 acres of land and a water right.
George Mortuck $1,001.00 4 ac 35-acre farm; 4 acres taken.

Sarah J. Nelson $600.00 — Smaller award.

Source: "The Awards," Brewster Standard, July 11, 1890. Sodom Dam proceeding (Chapter 490, Laws of 1883; 1887 map proceeding). This article covers the first major watershed proceeding affecting Southeast Centre — additional takings occurred in the Double Reservoir I proceeding (1896), the Brewster Sanitary proceeding (1893), and others through 1908.

Historical and contemporary maps documenting the neighborhood before and after the watershed transformation.

1867 Beers Atlas
F.W. Beers' Putnam County atlas, enlarged by Peter A. Keery. The only map showing Southeast Centre in its intact pre-watershed form — every property labeled by owner or resident.
1867

Current Tax Map
Town of Southeast Tax Map Section 57.18, Block 1. Shows the eleven surviving private lots alongside the City of New York watershed parcels that replaced most of the neighborhood.
Current
🛰
Aerial View
Southeast Centre from above. The Bog Brook Reservoir occupies the upper right; the East Branch Reservoir lies to the south and east. The two surviving private lots on Brewster Hill Road are visible at center.
Current
💧
Double Reservoir I Map (1896)
Map Number 799, filed May 14, 1896. Official City of New York acquisition map showing every parcel taken in the Double Reservoir I proceeding — including the Howes family upland farm parcels and the Yale Tract.
Filed May 14, 1896
🔜
1887 Watershed Appraisal Map
Maps filed May 17, 1887 under Chapter 490, Laws of 1883 — the first formal watershed appraisal proceeding affecting Southeast Centre.

Ask about Southeast Centre

Sample questions
SC
Hello! I'm your guide to Southeast Centre — the neighborhood at Sodom Road and Brewster Hill Road in the Town of Southeast, Putnam County. I know the deed research for all eleven properties in detail: ownership chains going back to the 1700s in some cases, the families who lived here, and the story of how the City of New York's reservoir system transformed this village. What would you like to know?
Southeast Centre · Town of Southeast · Putnam County, New York

A Village That Almost Disappeared

Tax Map Section 57.18, Block 1 · Primary deed research · 2025–2026

There is a spot in the Town of Southeast, Putnam County, New York, where three roads meet at the foot of a hill. For most of the nineteenth century it was known as Sodom Corners — a name that reflects more about local humor than local character. By mid-century it had acquired a more dignified identity: Southeast Centre, the geographic and civic heart of the town. Today it is a quiet junction enclosed on three sides by City of New York watershed land. The reservoirs that displaced its farms, its church, and most of its families are still there, full and cold, supplying water to New York City. What is left of the neighborhood fits on a few pages of the current tax roll.

The Ground It Stands On

The story begins with a man named Moody Howes. Born July 18, 1724, he moved to Southeast, New York in 1748 and, as H.H. Roberts wrote in his 1876 centennial address to the town, "bought all the land in its immediate vicinity." The 1892 Howes Family Genealogy confirms he "purchased land of Indians." From that founding purchase, everything in this neighborhood flows.

Moody's son Daniel, born 1763, married Ruhama Reed and settled at what the family called the Old Howes Place. When Daniel died in 1824, Ruhama outlived him by sixty years, dying in 1864 at ninety-one. Their children would shape the century to come. The Howes family did not merely live here — they were the ground itself.

The first town meeting of the Town of Southeast was held in 1796 at the home of Zalmon Sanford, which stood "at the meeting of three roads at the foot of Brewster Hill" — the same intersection. The road climbing north past the Howes estate was called Howes Street on the 1867 Beers atlas, and is now called Brewster Hill Road, named for an early settler on the hill. The road running east past the church and parsonage was Church Street, now Sodom Road. A fourth street — Foster Avenue — ran northeast from the intersection before the City of New York absorbed it into watershed land after 1896.

The Brothers Who Built It

Daniel and Ruhama Reed Howes had at least six sons. Three of them — Nathan, Jacob, and Seth — built Southeast Centre into what it became.

Nathan Alva Howes, born at the homestead on April 22, 1796, entered the circus trade in 1811 at age fifteen. His 1878 obituary called him "the father of the Exhibition business in this country." In 1825 he manufactured the first circus tent ever known, sewn on South Street in New York. He served as Southeast Town Supervisor, Justice of the Peace, and twice as State Senator. He married Clarissa Crosby — which explains the Crosby family's pervasive presence throughout the neighborhood's deed record as boundary neighbors and witnesses across three generations.

Jacob Orson Howes, born March 8, 1807, worked as a carpenter for twenty years, spent four years in the circus alongside his brothers, then returned to farm near Southeast Centre. Around 1856 he opened a lumber yard in Brewster with Edward Howes; their firm built the brick block that still stands on Main Street. He served as Deputy Collector of Internal Revenue for eight years. By 1874 he controlled more of Southeast Centre's economic infrastructure than any other single figure: six distinct land parcels, a lumber yard, the old Crane's grist mill on the Croton River that he had sold in 1836 and repurchased in 1865. He died on Sunday, May 7, 1876, at age sixty-nine, suddenly, "almost before Dr. Smith could be summoned from the Centre Church, where he happened to be at the time."

Seth Benedict Howes, born at the homestead on August 15, 1815, was the youngest and the most famous. He accompanied Nathan on the first elephant circus tour in 1826 at age eleven. He rose to become one of the great circus entrepreneurs of the nineteenth century. He made a fortune in Chicago real estate with partner Paul Cornell — buying land that would become Hyde Park alongside James Crane of Southeast, who had moved to Chicago as a merchant. He married Amy Mozley in London on January 25, 1867. They would not come to America together for another seventeen years. When they finally came, they came to Stonehenge — a Victorian mansion behind a dressed-stone wall at 10 Brewster Hill Road, the oldest continuous address in the neighborhood.

The 1867 Map and What It Shows

The 1867 F.W. Beers Atlas of Putnam County — enlarged by Peter A. Keery — is the essential visual anchor for this research. It is the only map to show Southeast Centre in its intact pre-watershed form, with every property identified by the name of its owner or resident. Every label on that map now has a confirmed deed-chain identity, traced forward through the watershed proceedings, the grantor index, and in most cases to the present owners.

Reading the map from north to south along Howes Street (Brewster Hill Road): J. Silk at the top, then A. Townsend, J.O.H., J.E. Reed, and W. Crosby — five labels that would become two modern lots. East of the road, set back and circled, sits J.O. Howes with two structure symbols: Jacob's upland farm, the ground the City would flood to build the East Branch Reservoir. At the intersection, the Carriage and Chair Factory of Wright & Company. South along Church Street (Sodom Road): D. Reed, then B.S.SH (the blacksmith shop), Mrs. Paddock, and a sequence of households running east toward the Presbyterian Church and the schoolhouse. West along Main Street: T. Knox, the Crosby parcels, George Cole's wagon shop, and at the river's edge, the mill pond loop that marks Crane's Mills — the grist mill Jacob sold in 1836 and bought back in 1865.

The Beers Business Directory confirms the economic character of the neighborhood at its peak: Burch & Beers — Manufacturers of Fur Hats; Geo. Cole — Carriage & Wagon Maker; S. Reed — Manufacturer of Boots & Shoes; Rogers & Hoyt — Tanners & Dealers in Leather. H.H. Roberts, in his 1876 address, called it "Hatesville" for the hat manufacturing concentration at the intersection. The Roberts address was written at the moment of the neighborhood's greatest prosperity — and also at the moment the watershed began to advance.

The Watershed

"The new Croton river dam and reservoir are being built, also, in the western part of the town," Roberts wrote in that same 1876 address, in the same paragraph where he was celebrating a century of local prosperity. Within twenty years, twenty-one families had been displaced from the Southeast Centre area and 1,471 acres had been absorbed into the City's holdings.

The proceedings moved through Southeast Centre in waves. The 1887 map proceeding (Chapter 490, Laws of 1883) took the upland farms east of Brewster Hill Road and the river tracts to the west. The Double Reservoir I proceeding (maps filed May 14, 1896) took the residual improved cores — including the farmhouse visible in the surviving photograph of the Howes family farm. The Brewster Sanitary proceeding (Chapter 189, Laws of 1893) took the west-of-highway Croton River parcels. By 1900 the City of New York had acquired everything that made Southeast Centre function as a working agricultural community.

The July 11, 1890 Brewster Standard published the commissioners' awards in full. They are a record of what was lost. Stephen C. Barnum received $61,179 for a three-generation farm of 250 acres with twenty or more buildings, a mill, and water power — he had claimed $182,000. Lydia Yale received $17,077.50 for the 130-acre farm her husband had paid $13,000 for in 1857 and improved over three decades. George Cole, whose wagon shop appears in the Beers Business Directory, "was driven out of his home and away from his business." Melissa Howes Birch — Jacob O. Howes's daughter, who had grown up on the 140-acre family farm — had refused an offer of $25,000 the year before the taking. She received $15,700 and built a new house in Brewster village with the proceeds.

The gap between what people believed their land was worth and what the commissioners awarded runs through every entry. Water power — the mill dams and races and privileges that had driven the neighborhood's economy since the 1740s — was the most contested element throughout. "This had proved to be a very knotty question throughout the whole proceeding," the newspaper observed.

What Remains

The East Branch Reservoir was put in service in 1891. The Carriage and Chair Factory was demolished in December 1889. The Presbyterian church — organized June 14, 1853, built 1853–54, dedicated in June 1854 — was sold to John R. Yale and James D. Baxter for $104 in 1891, converted to a schoolhouse, and is now a private home at 112 Sodom Road. The parsonage sits a few hundred feet away. The farmhouse in the Howes family photograph is gone — flooded, the obituary of Vilette Howes Birch records, along with "the destruction of the quaint old farm house with its broad roof, which sheltered a happy, contented family."

What remains is what this research documents: two Brewster Hill Road lots still in private hands, their 605-foot frontage anchored by a Victorian mansion and a stone wall Seth Howes built beginning in the summer of 1877. Nine Sodom Road properties where the structures still stand, their chains documented from the Commissioners of Forfeiture through the present owners. The Howes family monument at Milltown Cemetery: Quincy granite, twenty-four feet, three thousand dollars, four brothers — Seth, Reuben, Nathan, and Jacob — erected in the month of May 1876 when the family buried Jacob and his sister Fanny within eight days of each other.

Seth outlived them all. He moved to Morningthorpe — a stone castle on Turk Hill Road, named for the ancestral Howes family seat in Norfolk, England, which the family had held since 1186 and lost in 1883 — in July 1894. He died there on May 16, 1901. The stone church he funded in Brewster was dedicated twenty-eight days later. The dressed-stone wall at Stonehenge still stands. The reservoir is still there.

The Properties

Click a property on the map to explore its full deed history, key families, and chain of title. Eleven properties — two on Brewster Hill Road, nine on Sodom Road — documented from their earliest confirmed deed records to the present.
